# Introduction to Value Chain Mapping — Simon Wardley

- **Speaker:** Simon Wardley, researcher, former CEO/strategist
- **Event:** Conference talk (Open Source / Government context)
- **Format:** Lightning talk
- **Duration:** ~13 minutes

## Key Takeaways

Most business "strategies" are just copying what others do with buzzwords. Wardley Mapping provides actual situational awareness by combining value chains with evolution to create a visual map you can reason about.

> 1. **Most strategy documents are "blahs"** — strip out implementation details and you find vague buzzword soup. Wardley auto-generated 64 plausible strategies from a template and common buzzwords; people couldn't tell them from real ones.
> 2. **Maps require two axes: value chain (vertical) and evolution (horizontal)** — a value chain alone is useless because businesses don't stand still. Adding the evolution axis (genesis -> custom -> product -> commodity) gives context for decision-making.
> 3. **No one-size-fits-all management method** — Agile works in the uncharted/novel space, Six Sigma in the industrialized, Lean in the middle. A map tells you which method applies where.
> 4. **Evolution is predictable in direction but not timing** — you can't predict when something will commoditize, but you know it will. You can't predict what new things will appear, but you know they will once commoditization enables higher-order systems.
> 5. **Open means are a strategic weapon** — open source, open data, and open APIs accelerate evolution (move components left-to-right on the map). Companies using "open by purpose" outperformed those using "open by default."

## Speaker Background

Simon Wardley is a researcher and former strategist best known for creating Wardley Maps — a visual method for mapping business strategy based on value chains and evolution. He ran strategy for Canonical (Ubuntu) from 2008-2010, using mapping to achieve ~70% of the cloud OS market. He's a self-described "recovering management consultant" and scientist by training who brings an empirical, data-driven approach to strategy.

## Core Thesis

Business leaders lack **situational awareness** — the ability to see where they are relative to the landscape, the way a military general uses a geographical map. Box-and-wire diagrams (org charts, system diagrams) provide no context about evolution or competitive position. Wardley Mapping solves this by plotting value chain components against their evolutionary stage, creating a visual map that enables actual strategic reasoning: where to attack, what to outsource, what to build, and how to manipulate the competitive landscape.

## Major Topics Discussed

### [[00:00:59]](https://youtu.be/NnFeIt-uaEc?t=59) The Strategy Blah Generator

Wardley attended conferences, catalogued common strategy buzzwords ("digital business," "big data," "disruptive," "innovative," "ecosystem"), created a fill-in-the-blank template, and auto-generated 64 plausible strategy statements. When shared with 150 people, responses were: "this is more or less the exact wording from our business plan," "I've seen two of these used already," and "are you for hire?" This became "Strategy as a Service" — a GitHub repo that generates strategy documents on demand. The point: most strategy is backward causality and copying.

### [[00:05:01]](https://youtu.be/NnFeIt-uaEc?t=301) Why Maps Matter — Military Analogy

Generals don't bomb hills because 67% of other generals bomb hills. They use maps. Maps tell you **where** you can attack; **why** is a relative statement (why here over there). Then you get into the how, what, and when. In military terms, "where and why" is situational awareness; "how, what, when" is action. Business has lots of action but almost no situational awareness.

### [[00:05:47]](https://youtu.be/NnFeIt-uaEc?t=347) Building a Map — Value Chain + Evolution

**Value chain (vertical axis):** Start with a user need, then trace the chain of dependencies. Cup of tea -> tea + hot water -> water + kettle -> power. For a business: online image manipulation -> photo storage -> compute infrastructure, etc.

**Evolution (horizontal axis):** Components evolve through four stages driven by competition:
- **Genesis** — novel, chaotic, uncertain, unpredictable
- **Custom built** — bespoke solutions
- **Product/rental** — standardized offerings
- **Commodity/utility** — industrialized, ordered, measured, standard, dull

Example: computing went from Z3 (1943, genesis) -> custom systems (LEO) -> products (IBM 650) -> rental (timeshare) -> commodity hardware -> utility (cloud).

### [[00:07:50]](https://youtu.be/NnFeIt-uaEc?t=470) What Maps Enable

- **Method selection:** Agile for uncharted space, Six Sigma for industrialized, Lean for the middle. A map of the UK's 70 billion high-speed rail project shows which parts to outsource to utilities, build with off-the-shelf products, or build in-house with agile.
- **Scenario planning:** A security company can map out "what if identity becomes a commodity?" vs. "what if we create trust as a service?"
- **Pattern recognition:** Componentization (electricity enables radio, TV, computing), inertia from past success, the Peace-War-Wonder cycle.

### [[00:10:05]](https://youtu.be/NnFeIt-uaEc?t=605) Peace, War, and Wonder

A recurring economic pattern: peaceful competition -> big vendors build inertia -> someone commoditizes the industry -> disruption -> explosion of new higher-order systems (wonder). Companies get disrupted by this even though it's highly predictable — Douglas Parkhill wrote about cloud computing 48 years before it happened.

### [[00:10:57]](https://youtu.be/NnFeIt-uaEc?t=657) Manipulating the Map with Open

All components evolve through competition, but you can **accelerate** evolution using open means (open source, open data, open APIs). You can also slow it down through patents, FUD, or artificial constraints. Wardley's study of 160 companies showed that those with high strategic play who used "open by purpose" (deliberately manipulating markets) had strong 7-year market cap growth. Those with "open by default" (throwing things over the wall) did not.

### [[00:11:36]](https://youtu.be/NnFeIt-uaEc?t=696) Ubuntu's Cloud Strategy

At Canonical (2008), Wardley mapped the server OS market. They knew compute was moving to utility (cloud), predicted new practices would emerge and someone would build a platform. Instead of fighting Red Hat on the existing battlefield, they "decided to own the future and let the future catch up." By 2010, Ubuntu held ~70% of the cloud market.

## Books, Tools & Resources Mentioned

- **Wardley Maps** — the mapping technique itself (freely available at blog.gardeviance.org)
- **Strategy as a Service** — Wardley's satirical auto-strategy generator (on GitHub)
- **Douglas Parkhill** — wrote about cloud computing concepts 48 years before cloud became real
- **In Search of Excellence** (implied) — the 1984 business book advising companies to emulate 62 "excellent" companies (Kodak, Atari, etc.)
